title: 君主论
description: 君主论 — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: 'A knowledge base on Machiavelli''s "The Prince"'

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Complete table of contents for The Prince, including introduction, dedication, 26 chapters, and appendices'

memes.label: Achilles
memes.description: 'Legendary Greek hero of the Trojan War, cited by Machiavelli as an exemplar whom Alexander the Great imitated — and as a prince who was educated by the centaur Chiron, symbolizing the union of force and cunning.'

memes.label: Agathocles of Syracuse
memes.description: 'Agathocles (361–289 BC), tyrant of Syracuse and archetypal criminal prince in The Prince Chapter 8, who rose from low origins to kingship through calculated cruelty.'

memes.label: Clodius Albinus
memes.description: 'Roman usurper (c. 150–197 CE) who challenged Septimius Severus for the imperial throne, cited by Machiavelli as an example of a ruler deceived by promises and destroyed by his own hesitation.'

memes.label: Alexander the Great
memes.description: 'King of Macedon (r. 336–323 BCE) and conqueror of the Persian Empire, cited by Machiavelli as a model of military virtue and generosity for princes to imitate.'

memes.label: Ambition (Ambizione)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, ambition is the driving force of political action — both the source of greatness and the cause of ruin when unchecked by prudence or constrained by stable institutions.'

memes.label: Antiochus III
memes.description: 'Seleucid king (r. 223–187 BCE), cited by Machiavelli as an example of a ruler who lost his kingdom by failing to manage conquered territories and provoking Roman intervention.'

memes.label: "Appendix: Castruccio Castracani"
memes.description: "The Life of Castruccio Castracani of Lucca"

memes.label: "Appendix: Duke Valentino"
memes.description: "Description of the Methods Adopted by the Duke Valentino When Murdering Vitellozzo Vitelli, Oliverotto da Fermo, the Signor Pagolo, and the Duke di Gravina Orsini"

memes.label: Arms (Arme)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, a ruler who does not possess his own arms cannot be secure. Good arms are the indispensable foundation of good laws and the first principle of princely self-reliance.'

memes.label: Auxiliaries
memes.description: 'Troops lent by another power — Machiavelli argues they are even more dangerous than mercenaries because they are united and trained to obey someone else'

memes.label: Battle of Vaila
memes.description: 'The Battle of Vaila (Agnadello, 1509) — the decisive engagement of the War of the League of Cambrai in which Venice lost in a single day all that she had accumulated over eight hundred years.'

memes.label: Bologna
memes.description: 'A papal city ruled by the Bentivogli family — Machiavelli''s example of hereditary princely rule and the consequences of losing popular support'

memes.label: Caracalla
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 198–217 CE), son of Septimius Severus, cited by Machiavelli as an example of a ruler whose excessive cruelty toward his subjects led to his assassination.'

memes.label: Catherina Sforza
memes.description: "Countess of Forlì (1463–1509); the first diplomatic mission of Machiavelli was to her court, and from her conduct he drew the lesson that earning the confidence of the people is better than relying on fortresses."

memes.label: Cesare Borgia
memes.description: 'The son of Pope Alexander VI whose career as Duke of Romagna serves as Machiavelli''s central exemplar of the new prince who rises through fortune but acts with virtù'

memes.label: "Chapter I"
memes.description: "How Many Kinds of Principalities There Are, and by What Means They Are Acquired"

memes.label: "Chapter II"
memes.description: "Concerning Hereditary Principalities"

memes.label: "Chapter III"
memes.description: "Concerning Mixed Principalities"

memes.label: "Chapter IV"
memes.description: "Why the Kingdom of Darius, Conquered by Alexander, Did Not Rebel Against the Successors of Alexander at His Death"

memes.label: "Chapter V"
memes.description: "Concerning the Way to Govern Cities or Principalities Which Lived Under Their Own Laws Before They Were Annexed"

memes.label: "Chapter VI"
memes.description: "Concerning New Principalities Which Are Acquired by One's Own Arms and Ability"

memes.label: "Chapter VII"
memes.description: "Concerning New Principalities Which Are Acquired Either by the Arms of Others or by Good Fortune"

memes.label: "Chapter VIII"
memes.description: "Concerning Those Who Have Obtained a Principality by Wickedness"

memes.label: "Chapter IX"
memes.description: "Concerning a Civil Principality"

memes.label: "Chapter X"
memes.description: "Concerning the Way in Which the Strength of All Principalities Ought to Be Measured"

memes.label: "Chapter XI"
memes.description: "Concerning Ecclesiastical Principalities"

memes.label: "Chapter XII"
memes.description: "How Many Kinds of Soldiery There Are, and Concerning Mercenaries"

memes.label: "Chapter XIII"
memes.description: "Concerning Auxiliaries, Mixed Soldiery, and One's Own"

memes.label: "Chapter XIV"
memes.description: "That Which Concerns a Prince on the Subject of the Art of War"

memes.label: "Chapter XV"
memes.description: "Concerning Things for Which Men, and Especially Princes, Are Praised or Blamed"

memes.label: "Chapter XVI"
memes.description: "Concerning Liberality and Meanness"

memes.label: "Chapter XVII"
memes.description: "Concerning Cruelty and Clemency, and Whether It Is Better to Be Loved Than Feared"

memes.label: "Chapter XVIII"
memes.description: "Concerning the Way in Which Princes Should Keep Faith"

memes.label: "Chapter XIX"
memes.description: "That One Should Avoid Being Despised and Hated"

memes.label: "Chapter XX"
memes.description: "Are Fortresses, and Many Other Things to Which Princes Often Resort, Advantageous or Hurtful?"

memes.label: "Chapter XXI"
memes.description: "How a Prince Should Conduct Himself So as to Gain Renown"

memes.label: "Chapter XXII"
memes.description: "Concerning the Secretaries of Princes"

memes.label: "Chapter XXIII"
memes.description: "How Flatterers Should Be Avoided"

memes.label: "Chapter XXIV"
memes.description: "Why the Princes of Italy Have Lost Their States"

memes.label: "Chapter XXV"
memes.description: "What Fortune Can Effect in Human Affairs and How to Withstand Her"

memes.label: "Chapter XXVI"
memes.description: "An Exhortation to Liberate Italy from the Barbarians"

memes.label: Charles VIII of France
memes.description: 'King of France (r. 1483–1498); his 1494 invasion of Italy opened the peninsula to foreign intervention and his weak conduct closed every door to French influence, forcing his successor Louis XII to rebuild from nothing.'

memes.label: Civil Principality
memes.description: 'A principality where a private citizen becomes prince through the favour of fellow-citizens rather than through violence or hereditary succession.'

memes.label: Clemency (Clemenza)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, clemency is a princely virtue that must be used with caution — misused clemency allows disorders to flourish, while well-measured severity preserves order.'

memes.label: Colonies
memes.description: 'In The Prince, sending colonies to conquered territories is Machiavelli''s preferred method of maintaining control — cheaper and more effective than maintaining armed garrisons.'

memes.label: Commodus
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 177–192 CE), cited by Machiavelli in Chapter 19 as an example of a ruler whose cruelty made him hated and led to his assassination.'

memes.label: Conspiracy (Congiura)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, conspiracy is the mechanism through which internal enemies seek to overthrow a ruler — and the ultimate test of whether a prince has avoided hatred.'

memes.label: Cruelty
memes.description: 'Machiavelli''s distinction between cruelty "well-used" — inflicted once, for necessity, and not repeated — and cruelty abused, a core doctrine of The Prince concerning the ethics of princely violence'

memes.label: Cyrus the Great
memes.description: 'Founder of the Achaemenid Persian Empire (r. 559–530 BCE), cited by Machiavelli as one of the four great armed prophets who rose by their own ability.'

memes.label: David
memes.description: 'Biblical king of Israel, cited by Machiavelli in Chapter 13 as an exemplar of a ruler who fought with his own arms rather than borrowed weapons.'

memes.label: "Dedication"
memes.description: "Dedication to Lorenzo de' Medici"

memes.label: Didius Julianus
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 193 CE) who bought the throne at auction and reigned nine weeks before being killed by Septimius Severus, cited by Machiavelli as proof that weakness invites destruction.'

memes.label: Duke of Gravina
memes.description: 'Francesco Orsini, Duke of Gravina, an Italian nobleman and condottiero who was among the conspirators killed by Cesare Borgia at the Sinigaglia massacre in 1502.'

memes.label: Ecclesiastical Principality
memes.description: 'The type of principality ruled by the Church — unique in Machiavelli''s typology because it requires neither virtue nor fortune to maintain, being sustained by "ancient religious institutions" alone'

memes.label: Faith / Faithlessness
memes.description: 'Machiavelli''s doctrine that a prudent prince should know how to keep faith when it benefits him and break it when necessary, while always appearing faithful.'

memes.label: Fear vs. Love
memes.description: 'Machiavelli''s doctrine that a prince should prefer being feared over loved when both cannot be maintained, but must avoid being hated.'
aliases: whether it is better to be feared than loved

memes.label: Ferdinand of Aragon
memes.description: 'King of Spain (r. 1479–1516); presented by Machiavelli as the exemplar of a prince who achieves greatness through the cloak of religion while possessing none of its virtues.'

memes.label: Fermo
memes.description: "A city in the Marche region of Italy, known in The Prince as the setting of Oliverotto da Fermo's treacherous seizure of power, analyzed in Chapter 8 as an example of crime-based principality acquisition."

memes.label: Ferrara
memes.description: 'A duchy ruled by the House of Este — Machiavelli''s example of a hereditary principality that survived foreign invasions through ancient lineage and strategic balancing'

memes.label: Flatterers
memes.description: 'In The Prince, flatterers pose a subtle danger to rulers — the only defense is to choose wise counselors and let them speak truth without fear.'

memes.label: Florence
memes.description: 'Florence, the Renaissance city-state and republic, was Machiavellis birthplace and the political laboratory from which he drew his most acute observations on republican and princely government.'

memes.label: Forli
memes.description: "A city in the Romagna region, noted in The Prince as the domain of Catherina Sforza and a target of Cesare Borgia's military campaign, illustrating the vulnerabilities of mercenary-dependent defenses."

memes.label: Fortresses
memes.description: 'Machiavelli''s analysis of whether fortresses are useful to a prince — concluding that the best fortress is not to be hated by the people.'

memes.label: Fortuna
memes.description: 'Machiavelli''s concept of fortune — the unpredictable force that governs half of human affairs and which the prince must learn to resist and channel through virtù'

memes.label: Foundations (Fondamenti)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, good foundations are the deep structures — good laws and good arms — upon which all durable principalities are built and without which even the greatest virtù cannot long sustain a state.'

memes.label: France
memes.description: 'The Kingdom of France under Louis XII, the dominant foreign power in Italian affairs during Machiavellis career, whose errors in Italy provide the cautionary case study of Chapter 3 of The Prince.'

memes.label: Francesco Sforza
memes.description: 'The condottiero who became Duke of Milan — Machiavelli''s exemplar of the prince who rises by his own arms and ability'

memes.label: Fraud (Frode)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, fraud is one of the two modes of animal combat — the fox''s method — and a legitimate tool of statecraft when used to overcome enemies and secure the state.'

memes.label: French Invasion of 1494
memes.description: 'The invasion of Italy by Charles VIII of France in 1494 — the event that shattered the Italian balance of power and opened the peninsula to decades of foreign intervention, forming the backdrop to The Prince'

memes.label: Genoa
memes.description: 'A major maritime republic of Renaissance Italy, whose shifting allegiance between France and the Italian powers is noted in The Prince as an example of the consequences of foreign intervention.'

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: 'The German lands of the Holy Roman Empire, analyzed in The Prince as exemplifying a distinctive political constitution of free cities and princely states that makes them difficult to conquer.'

memes.label: Giampaolo Baglioni
memes.description: 'Tyrant of Perugia who conspired against Cesare Borgia at the Magione meeting'

memes.label: Giorgio Scali
memes.description: 'Florentine demagogue mentioned in Chapter 9 as an example of one who rose through popular favor'

memes.label: Giovanni Bentivogli
memes.description: "Ruler of Bologna (1443–1508); his family survived assassination through popular goodwill, illustrating the principle of Machiavelli that the favor of the people is the best fortress of a prince."

memes.label: Giovanni Fogliani
memes.description: 'Maternal uncle of Oliverotto da Fermo, murdered by his nephew at the Banquet of Fermo in 1501, an episode Machiavelli recounts in Chapter 8 as an example of criminal treachery.'

memes.label: Girolamo Savonarola
memes.description: 'Florentine Dominican friar and reformer (1452–1498); cited in The Prince as the definitive example of an "unarmed prophet" who comes to a bad end.'

memes.label: Glory (Gloria)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, glory is the ultimate reward of princely virtù — won through great deeds, new foundations, and successful arms.'

memes.label: Greece
memes.description: 'Ancient Greece as a political reference point in The Prince, used by Machiavelli to illustrate the dynamics of foreign intervention, coalition warfare, and the structural weaknesses of divided states.'

memes.label: Hatred (Odio)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, hatred is the one sentiment a prince must above all avoid — more dangerous than being feared, and the surest path to ruin.'

memes.label: Heliogabalus
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 218–222), mentioned in Chapter 19 as an example of contemptible rulers'

memes.label: Hereditary Principality
memes.description: 'A principality ruled by a long-established dynasty — the easiest type of state to maintain according to Machiavelli'

memes.label: "Introduction"
memes.description: "Translator's introduction to Machiavelli's life and works"

memes.label: Italy
memes.description: 'The central theater of The Prince — a politically fragmented peninsula contested by France, Spain, the papacy, and local princes'

memes.label: Justice (Giustizia)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, justice is instrumental rather than absolute — a just war is one that is necessary, and justice in rule serves the stability of the state rather than an abstract moral order.'

memes.label: Law (Legge)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, law is one half of the centaur — the human mode of rule, paired with force. Good laws depend on good arms, and both sustain the state.'

memes.label: League of Cambrai
memes.description: 'The League of Cambrai (1508–1510), an anti-Venetian alliance of European powers that Machiavelli analyzes as a case study in the fragility of coalitions and the perils of strengthening ones rivals.'
aliases: War of the League of Cambrai

memes.label: Liberality
memes.description: 'Machiavellis analysis of liberality (generosity) as a princely virtue that paradoxically leads to ruin when practiced, and the superior alternative of parsimony.'

memes.label: Liberty (Libertà)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, liberty (libertà) is the cherished political condition of free republics — a fragile state that makes conquest difficult but which, once lost, is nearly impossible to restore without destroying the conquered state.'

memes.label: Lion and Fox
memes.description: 'In The Prince, the lion and the fox are the two natures a prince must combine — the lion frightens wolves (force), the fox recognizes snares (cunning). Neither alone is sufficient.'

memes.label: Lombardy
memes.description: 'A wealthy northern Italian region contested by France, Venice, and local princes — central to Machiavelli''s analysis of Louis XII''s errors in Italy'

memes.label: Lorenzo de' Medici
memes.description: 'Ruler of Florence (1449–1492), known as "Il Magnifico"; the youth of Machiavelli was shaped by his governance and his grandson was the dedicatee of The Prince.'

memes.label: Louis XI
memes.description: 'King of France (r. 1461–1483); father of Jeanne of France, whose marriage dissolution by Louis XII was a condition of papal support.'

memes.label: Louis XII of France
memes.description: 'King of France (r. 1498–1515), called "The Father of the People"; a central negative example in The Prince whose five errors in Italian statecraft cost him Lombardy.'

memes.label: Lucca
memes.description: 'A Tuscan city-state and the birthplace of Castruccio Castracani, appearing in The Prince as both a political actor in Chapter 7 and the central setting of the biographical appendix.'

memes.label: Macrinus
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 217–218), briefly mentioned in Chapter 19 among contemptible rulers'

memes.label: Marcus Aurelius
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 161–180 CE) and Stoic philosopher, cited by Machiavelli in Chapter 19 as the model of a virtuous ruler who succeeded because he inherited the empire rather than seizing it.'

memes.label: Maximilian I
memes.description: 'Holy Roman Emperor (r. 1493–1519); portrayed by Machiavelli as a secretive ruler whose refusal to seek or heed counsel caused his repeated failures.'
aliases: Maximilian I of the Holy Roman Empire

memes.label: Maximinus
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 235–238 CE), cited by Machiavelli in Chapter 19 as a cruel and warlike ruler killed by his own soldiers.'

memes.label: Mercenaries
memes.description: 'Machiavelli''s analysis of mercenary arms as useless and dangerous — soldiers who fight only for pay and have no loyalty to the prince or state'

memes.label: Milan
memes.description: 'The Duchy of Milan — a Lombard city-state that Machiavelli uses as the exemplar of a principality acquired through both fortune and ability, seized by Francesco Sforza and repeatedly contested by France'

memes.label: Mixed Principality
memes.description: 'A principality formed by annexing a new territory to an existing state — the most unstable form of rule in Machiavelli''s typology, requiring the prince to navigate contested loyalties and structural resistance'

memes.label: Moses
memes.description: 'Biblical lawgiver and leader of the Israelites, cited by Machiavelli as the supreme example of an armed prophet who founded a new order through his own ability.'

memes.label: Nabis
memes.description: 'Tyrant of Sparta (r. 207–192 BCE), cited by Machiavelli as an example of a ruler who maintained power by keeping the people satisfied while eliminating his rivals.'

memes.label: Naples
memes.description: 'The Kingdom of Naples — southern Italy''s hereditary monarchy, repeatedly conquered and divided by France and Spain in the Italian Wars'

memes.label: Native Troops
memes.description: 'Troops raised from a prince''s own subjects — the only reliable foundation of military power according to Machiavelli, who considered native arms the decisive factor separating successful princes from those who lost their states'

memes.label: Necessity (Necessità)
memes.description: 'A core Machiavellian concept: necessity (necessità) as the driving force that compels, justifies, and constrains princely action.'

memes.label: Neutrality (Neutralità)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, neutrality in the conflicts of others is almost always a mistake — a prince who declares himself decisively wins respect; one who remains neutral becomes the prey of the victor.'

memes.label: New Principality
memes.description: 'A principality newly acquired by a prince — the central subject of The Prince and the most difficult type of state to maintain'

memes.label: Niccolò Machiavelli
memes.description: 'Florentine diplomat, historian, and political philosopher (1469–1527); author of The Prince and secretary to the Florentine Republic.'

memes.label: Occasione (Opportunity)
memes.description: 'The Machiavellian concept that virtù requires opportunity (occasione) to manifest — great founders are those who recognize and seize the favorable moment.'

memes.label: Oliverotto da Fermo
memes.description: 'Oliverotto da Fermo (1475–1503), a condottiere who seized Fermo by murdering his uncle and leading citizens at a banquet — Machiavelli''s exemplar of criminal principality and its ultimate failure.'

memes.label: Orsini Family
memes.description: 'The Orsini, one of the great Roman baronial families whose conspiratorial opposition to Cesare Borgia — and subsequent elimination at Sinigaglia — provides a central case study in The Princes analysis of faction management.'

memes.label: Pagolo Vitelli
memes.description: 'Italian condottiero captain of the Florentine army, cited by Machiavelli as both the commander under whom Oliverotto da Fermo trained and an example of a prudent military leader.'

memes.label: Pandolfo Petrucci
memes.description: 'Ruler of Siena (r. 1487–1512); cited in The Prince as an exemplar of a prince who governs through those he once distrusted and of a ruler wise enough to choose a capable minister.'

memes.label: Papal States
memes.description: 'The temporal domain of the Pope in central Italy — Machiavelli''s analysis of ecclesiastical principalities as uniquely secure because they are sustained by religious authority rather than arms'

memes.label: Pertinax
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 193 CE) whose brief reign of three months ended in assassination, cited by Machiavelli as proof that excessive moderation can be fatal for a prince.'

memes.label: Perugia
memes.description: 'An Umbrian city ruled by the Baglioni family — the site of the Magione conspiracy against Cesare Borgia and a case study in failed princely ruthlessness'

memes.label: Pescennius Niger
memes.description: 'Roman usurper (c. 135–194 CE) who claimed the imperial throne against Septimius Severus, cited by Machiavelli as the first rival Severus defeated in his calculated campaign to secure sole power.'

memes.label: Philip II of Macedon
memes.description: 'King of Macedon (r. 359–336 BCE), father of Alexander the Great, cited by Machiavelli as an exemplar of a ruler who organized his own armed forces.'

memes.label: Philopoemen
memes.description: 'Greek general and strategos of the Achaean League (253–183 BCE), called "the last of the Greeks," cited by Machiavelli in Chapter 14 as the model of a prince who never ceases to study the art of war.'

memes.label: Pisa
memes.description: 'A Tuscan city-state and longtime rival of Florence, repeatedly discussed in The Prince as an example of resistance to conquest and the difficulty of holding acquired territories.'

memes.label: Pistoia
memes.description: 'A Tuscan city-state whose factional politics are analyzed in The Prince as a case study in how a ruler should manage internal divisions in subject territories.'

memes.label: Pope Alexander VI
memes.description: 'Rodrigo Borgia (1431–1503), Pope Alexander VI, whose pontificate exemplified ecclesiastical principality and whose ambitious state-building through his son Cesare Borgia forms a central case study in The Prince.'

memes.label: Pope Julius II
memes.description: 'Pope Julius II (Giuliano della Rovere, 1443–1513), the "Warrior Pope" — a central figure in Machiavelli''s analysis of ecclesiastical power, impetuous action, and the relationship between fortune and audacity.'

memes.label: Pope Leo X
memes.description: 'Medici pope (r. 1513–1521) who created cardinals and was the dedicatee of The Prince'

memes.label: Principality
memes.description: 'The core political unit analyzed in Machiavelli''s The Prince — a state ruled by a single prince, distinguished from a republic'

memes.label: Prudence (Prudenza)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, prudence is the faculty of knowing how to distinguish between courses of action and choose the one that avoids greater evils — the essential cognitive virtue of the successful prince.'

memes.label: Ramiro d'Orco
memes.description: 'A cruel but effective governor appointed by Cesare Borgia in the Romagna, later executed and displayed publicly to appease the people.'

memes.label: Republic
memes.description: 'The form of government by which a free people rules itself — the alternative to princely rule that Machiavelli served, admired, and analyzed as a distinct political species throughout his career'

memes.label: Republic of Venice
memes.description: 'The Most Serene Republic of Venice — the maritime republic that Machiavelli cites as an example of a state founded on ancient institutions, and whose downfall at Vaila he uses to warn against reliance on mercenary arms'

memes.label: Romagna
memes.description: 'The region of northern Italy that Cesare Borgia conquered and pacified — Machiavelli''s case study in the effective governance of a newly acquired territory'

memes.label: Rome
memes.description: 'The seat of the papacy and the model of ancient republican virtue — central to Machiavelli''s political imagination as both historical exemplar and contemporary power center'

memes.label: Romulus
memes.description: 'Legendary founder and first king of Rome, cited by Machiavelli as one of the four great armed prophets who rose by their own ability, alongside Moses, Cyrus, and Theseus.'

memes.label: Saul
memes.description: 'First king of Israel in the Old Testament, cited by Machiavelli in Chapter 13 as the ruler who mistakenly armed David with borrowed weapons — an allegory for the danger of relying on the arms of others.'

memes.label: Scipio Africanus
memes.description: 'Roman general (236–183 BCE) who defeated Hannibal, cited by Machiavelli as an exemplar of military virtue and the dangers of excessive clemency.'

memes.label: Septimius Severus
memes.description: 'Roman emperor (r. 193–211 CE), cited by Machiavelli in Chapter 19 as an exemplar of a ruler who combined the lion and the fox to maintain power.'

memes.label: Siena
memes.description: 'A Tuscan city-state whose prince Pandolfo Petrucci is cited in The Prince as an exemplar of prudent rule through trusted ministers, and which yielded to Cesare Borgia alongside Lucca.'

memes.label: Sinigaglia Massacre
memes.description: 'The 1502 trap in which Cesare Borgia eliminated his chief rivals — the most dramatic demonstration of Machiavellian virtù in The Prince'

memes.label: Spain
memes.description: 'Rising European power whose king Ferdinand of Aragon established dominance in southern Italy and rivaled France for control of the peninsula'

memes.label: Swiss Cantons
memes.description: 'The Swiss Cantons, whose infantry formations dominated Renaissance battlefields and whose role as both mercenary suppliers and independent military power provides Machiavelli with a key example in his analysis of arms and state power.'

memes.label: Theseus
memes.description: 'Legendary founder-king of Athens, cited by Machiavelli as one of the four great armed prophets who rose by their own ability, alongside Moses, Cyrus, and Romulus.'

memes.label: Tuscany
memes.description: 'A central Italian region dominated by Florence — the theater of Cesare Borgia''s ambitions and the home of Machiavelli''s political world'

memes.label: Urbino
memes.description: 'A duchy in the Marche region of Italy, conquered by Cesare Borgia and cited in The Prince as an example of both successful conquest and subsequent rebellion against papal authority.'

memes.label: Venice
memes.description: 'The Most Serene Republic — Italy''s leading maritime power and an example of aristocratic republican government analyzed in The Prince'

memes.label: Virtù
memes.description: 'Machiavelli''s concept of proactive ability — the skill, strength, and ingenuity a prince must possess to acquire and maintain a state'

memes.label: Vitellozzo Vitelli
memes.description: 'Italian condottiero and lord of Città di Castello; one of the captains strangled by Cesare Borgia at the massacre of Sinigaglia in 1502.'

memes.label: Wisdom (Sapienza)
memes.description: 'In The Prince, wisdom is not innate but arises from the quality of the prince''s counselors — a wise prince chooses wise men and knows how to use their counsel.'
